claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 14, and Blake who is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me ask a few questions about each child to assess your eligibility.

Let's start with some general questions:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now let me ask about your children.

For Alex (aged 14):
Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep (for example, on food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. They live with my ex-partner, and my ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since your ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, you would not be able to claim for Alex.

Now, let's discuss Blake (aged 19):

Is Blake currently in approved education or training? This would include things like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or other similar qualifications - but not university degrees or apprenticeships.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is studying for their A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. A-levels at sixth form college counts as approved education, which is good for eligibility.

Just a couple more questions about Blake:

1. Does Blake live with you, or if not, do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One more question about Blake:

Does Blake work 24 hours or more per week in paid employment, or receive any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work that many hours and doesn't receive any benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

### **Alex (aged 14) – Not Eligible**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex lives with your ex-partner, not with you
- Your ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex
- Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child

---

### **Blake (aged 19) – Eligible ✓**
You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- You live in the UK
- Blake lives with you
- Blake is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college)
- Blake does not work 24 or more hours per week
- Blake does not receive benefits in their own right

---

If you'd like to make a claim for Blake, you can do so through the official government channels. Please note that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit Tax Charge.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you. Additionally, your ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex. Since only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, and your ex-partner is already claiming, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old, which is over 16 but under 20. Blake is in approved education (studying A-levels at sixth form college), lives with you, does not work 24 or more hours per week, and does not receive benefits in their own right. You live in the UK and are responsible for Blake. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK and have two children. For Alex (aged 14), you are not eligible because Alex lives with your ex-partner who already claims Child Benefit for them - only one person can claim for each child. For Blake (aged 19), you are eligible because Blake lives with you, is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), does not work 24+ hours per week, and does not receive benefits in their own right.

Since only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, and your ex-partner is already claiming, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old, which is over 16 but under 20. Blake is in approved education (studying A-levels at sixth form college), lives with you, does not work 24 or more hours per week, and does not receive benefits in their own right. You live in the UK and are responsible for Blake. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK and have two children. For Alex (aged 14), you are not eligible because Alex lives with your ex-partner who already claims Child Benefit for them - only one person can claim for each child. For Blake (aged 19), you are eligible because Blake lives with you, is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), does not work 24+ hours per week, and does not receive benefits in their own right.
